From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Edible oilseed obtained from poppy

"Posto" redirects here. For the 2017 film, see [Posto (film)](/wiki/Posto_(film) "Posto (film)").

For the obsolete unit of length, see [English units of measurement](/wiki/English_units_of_measurement "English units of measurement").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/69/Poppy_seeds.jpg/250px-Poppy_seeds.jpg)](/wiki/File:Poppy_seeds.jpg)

Black poppy seeds in bulk

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/Mohn_z06.jpg/250px-Mohn_z06.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mohn_z06.jpg)

Dry poppy seed pods containing ripe poppy seeds

**Poppy seed** is an [oilseed](/wiki/Oilseed "Oilseed") obtained from the [poppy](/wiki/Poppy "Poppy") plant (*[Papaver somniferum](/wiki/Papaver_somniferum "Papaver somniferum")*). The tiny, kidney-shaped seeds have been harvested from dried [seed pods](/wiki/Seed_pods "Seed pods") by various civilizations for thousands of years. It is still widely used in many countries, especially in [Central Europe](/wiki/Central_Europe "Central Europe") and [South Asia](/wiki/South_Asia "South Asia"), where it is legally grown, used in food products and sold in shops. The seeds are used whole or ground into meal as an ingredient in many foods – especially in [pastry](/wiki/Pastry "Pastry") and [bread](/wiki/Bread "Bread") – and they are pressed to yield [poppyseed oil](/wiki/Poppyseed_oil "Poppyseed oil").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Poppy_seed&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/84/Poppies-seeds-dry.png/250px-Poppies-seeds-dry.png)](/wiki/File:Poppies-seeds-dry.png)

Dried poppy seed pods with stems attached ([poppy straws](/wiki/Poppy_straw "Poppy straw")), and seeds (in bowl)

The poppy seed is mentioned in ancient medical texts from many civilizations. For instance, the Egyptian [Ebers Papyrus](/wiki/Ebers_Papyrus "Ebers Papyrus"), written c. 1550 BCE, lists the poppy seed as a [sedative](/wiki/Sedative "Sedative").[[1]](#cite_note-1) The [Minoan civilization](/wiki/Minoan_civilization "Minoan civilization") (approximately 2700 to 1450 BCE), a Bronze Age civilization which arose on the island of [Crete](/wiki/Crete "Crete"), cultivated poppies for their seeds, and used a milk, opium and honey mixture to calm crying babies. The [Sumerians](/wiki/Sumer "Sumer") are another civilization that are known to have grown poppy seeds.[[2]](#cite_note-food-2)

## Description

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Poppy_seed&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Description")]

Poppy seeds are less than a millimeter in length, kidney-shaped, and have a pitted surface.[[3]](#cite_note-3) It takes about 3,300 poppy seeds to make up a gram, and between 1 and 2 million seeds to make up a pound.[[2]](#cite_note-food-2) The primary flavor compound is 2-pentylfuran.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

The seeds are cultivated for the flowers they produce. Annual and biennial poppies are used to cultivate from seed as they are not difficult to [propagate](/wiki/Plant_propagation "Plant propagation"), and can be put directly in the ground during winter.[[5]](#cite_note-5) The [California poppy](/wiki/California_poppy "California poppy") (*Eschscholzia californica*), for example, is a striking orange [wildflower](/wiki/Wildflower "Wildflower") that grows in the Western and Northwestern United States.

## Production and export

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Poppy_seed&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Production and export")]

The poppy seeds harvest can be a [by-product](/wiki/By-product "By-product") of cultivation of *[Papaver somniferum](/wiki/Papaver_somniferum "Papaver somniferum")* for [opium](/wiki/Opium "Opium"), [poppy straw](/wiki/Poppy_straw "Poppy straw"), or both opium and poppy straw. However, harvesting for poppy seeds of superior quality is in conflict with harvesting for opium as poppy seeds should be harvested when they are ripe, after the seed pod has dried. Traditionally, opium is harvested while the seed pods are green and the seeds have just begun to grow and their latex is abundant. Poppy straw can be a by-product of cultivation of poppy seeds. Compared to the seed pod and straw, the seeds contain very low levels of [opiates](/wiki/Opiates "Opiates").[[6]](#cite_note-erowid-6) The seeds may be washed to obtain [poppy tea](/wiki/Poppy_tea "Poppy tea") but a large amount is needed, around 300–400 g depending on the levels of opiates.

Since poppy seeds are relatively expensive, they are sometimes mixed with the seeds of *[Amaranthus paniculatus](/wiki/Amaranth "Amaranth")*, which closely resemble poppy seeds.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

In 2024, Turkey produced 8,441 [tonnes](/wiki/Tonne "Tonne") and [Serbia](/wiki/Serbia "Serbia") 1,079 tonnes.[[8]](#cite_note-faostat-8) The [Czech Republic](/wiki/Czech_Republic "Czech Republic") was the leading country exporting poppy seeds in 2024 with 23,600 tonnes (approximately 53 million lbs), followed by Hungary, the Netherlands, and Turkey.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

Poppy seeds are 6% water, 28% [carbohydrates](/wiki/Carbohydrates "Carbohydrates"), 42% [fat](/wiki/Fat "Fat"), and 21% [protein](/wiki/Protein_(nutrient) "Protein (nutrient)") (table). The fat content is primarily from the [polyunsaturated fat](/wiki/Polyunsaturated_fat "Polyunsaturated fat"), [linoleic acid](/wiki/Linoleic_acid "Linoleic acid"), an [omega-6 fatty acid](/wiki/Omega-6_fatty_acid "Omega-6 fatty acid") (source in table).

In a reference amount of 100 g (3.5 oz), poppy seeds provide 525 [calories](/wiki/Calories "Calories"), and are a rich source of [thiamin](/wiki/Thiamin "Thiamin"), [folate](/wiki/Folate "Folate"), and several [dietary minerals](/wiki/Mineral_(nutrient) "Mineral (nutrient)"), including [calcium](/wiki/Calcium "Calcium"), [iron](/wiki/Iron "Iron"), [magnesium](/wiki/Magnesium "Magnesium"), [manganese](/wiki/Manganese "Manganese"), [phosphorus](/wiki/Phosphorus "Phosphorus"), and [zinc](/wiki/Zinc "Zinc") (each having 20% or more of the [Daily Value](/wiki/Daily_Value "Daily Value"), table).

## Food products

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Poppy_seed&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Food products")]

### Intact seeds

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Poppy_seed&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Intact seeds")]

Whole poppy seeds are widely used as a spice and decoration in and on top of many baked goods and pastries. In [North America](/wiki/North_America "North America") they are used in and on many food items such as [poppyseed muffins](/wiki/Poppyseed_muffin "Poppyseed muffin"), [rusk](/wiki/Rusk "Rusk"), [bagels](/wiki/Bagel "Bagel") (like the [Montreal-style bagel](/wiki/Montreal-style_bagel "Montreal-style bagel")), [bialys](/wiki/Bialy_(pastry) "Bialy (pastry)"), and cakes such as [sponge cake](/wiki/Sponge_cake "Sponge cake"). Poppy seeds can also be used like [sesame](/wiki/Sesame "Sesame") seeds, added to hamburger buns or to make a bar of candy. The bars are made from boiled seeds mixed with sugar or with honey. This is especially common in the [Balkans](/wiki/Balkan "Balkan"), Greece and even in the cuisines of former Austro-Hungarian countries.

The color of poppy seeds is important in some uses. According to *[The Joy of Cooking](/wiki/The_Joy_of_Cooking "The Joy of Cooking")*, "the most desirable come from [Holland](/wiki/The_Netherlands "The Netherlands") and are a slate-blue color."[[12]](#cite_note-12) When used as a thickener in some dishes, white poppy seeds are preferred, having less impact on the color of the food. In other dishes, black poppy seeds are preferred, for maximum impact. Blue poppy seeds are used in various German breads and desserts as well as in [Polish cuisine](/wiki/Polish_cuisine "Polish cuisine").

### Paste

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Poppy_seed&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Paste")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/77/Poppy_seed_grinder_Jupiter_560%2C_in_operation.jpg/250px-Poppy_seed_grinder_Jupiter_560%2C_in_operation.jpg)](/wiki/File:Poppy_seed_grinder_Jupiter_560,_in_operation.jpg)

Grinding poppy seeds

Poppy seeds can be ground using a generic tool such as a [mortar and pestle](/wiki/Mortar_and_pestle "Mortar and pestle") or a small domestic type electric [blade grinder](/wiki/Blade_grinder "Blade grinder"), or a special purpose poppy seed grinder. A poppy seed grinder (mill) is a type of [burr grinder](/wiki/Burr_grinder "Burr grinder") with a set aperture that is too narrow for intact poppy seeds to pass through. A burr grinder produces a more uniform and less oily paste than these other tools.

The [poppy seed paste](/wiki/Poppy_seed_filling "Poppy seed filling") is used for fillings in pastries, sometimes mixed with butter or milk and sugar. The ground filling is used in [poppy seed rolls](/wiki/Poppy_seed_roll "Poppy seed roll") and some croissants and may be flavored with lemon or orange zest, [rum](/wiki/Rum "Rum") and [vanilla](/wiki/Vanilla "Vanilla") with raisins, heavy cream, cinnamon, and chopped blanched almonds or walnuts added. For sweet baked goods, sometimes instead of sugar a tablespoon of jam, or other sweet binding agent, like syrup is substituted. The poppy seed for fillings are best when they are finely and freshly ground because this will make a big difference in the pastry filling's texture and taste.

Poppy seed paste is available commercially, in cans. Poppy seeds are very high in oil, so commercial pastes normally contain sugar, water, and an emulsifier such as [soy lecithin](/wiki/Soy_lecithin "Soy lecithin") to keep the paste from separating. Commercial pastes also contain food preservatives to keep them from becoming rancid.

In the United States, commercial pastes are marketed under brand names including [Solo](/wiki/Solo_Foods "Solo Foods") and American Almond. Per 30 gram serving, the American Almond poppy seed paste has 120 calories, 4.5 grams fat, and 2 grams protein.

### Oil

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Poppy_seed&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Oil")]

Poppy seeds are pressed to form [poppyseed oil](/wiki/Poppyseed_oil "Poppyseed oil"), a valuable commercial oil that has multiple culinary and industrial uses.

### Other uses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Poppy_seed&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Other uses")]

Poppy seeds are often used as bird seed, in which case they are usually called maw seeds.[[13]](#cite_note-Ward-13)

## Use by cuisine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Poppy_seed&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Use by cuisine")]

See also: [List of poppy seed pastries and dishes](/wiki/List_of_poppy_seed_pastries_and_dishes "List of poppy seed pastries and dishes")

Poppy seeds are used around the world in various [cuisines](/wiki/Cuisine "Cuisine").

### European cuisine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Poppy_seed&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: European cuisine")]

See also: [Poppy seed filling](/wiki/Poppy_seed_filling "Poppy seed filling")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d8/Tuerkischer_Mohnkuchen.jpg/250px-Tuerkischer_Mohnkuchen.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tuerkischer_Mohnkuchen.jpg)

Turkish poppy-seed [cake](/wiki/Cake "Cake")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/Makov%C3%BD_kol%C3%A1%C4%8Dek.jpg/250px-Makov%C3%BD_kol%C3%A1%C4%8Dek.jpg)](/wiki/File:Makov%C3%BD_kol%C3%A1%C4%8Dek.jpg)

Czech blue poppy-filled [cake](/wiki/Kolach_(cake) "Kolach (cake)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bf/Mohnstollen.JPG/250px-Mohnstollen.JPG)](/wiki/File:Mohnstollen.JPG)

German [Mohnstollen](/wiki/Mohnstollen "Mohnstollen")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f7/Makowiec.jpg/250px-Makowiec.jpg)](/wiki/File:Makowiec.jpg)

[Polish](/wiki/Poland "Poland") [makowiec](/wiki/Makowiec_(pastry) "Makowiec (pastry)"), [Slovak](/wiki/Slovakia "Slovakia") makovník, a [nut roll](/wiki/Nut_roll "Nut roll") filled with poppy seed paste

Across Europe, [buns](/wiki/Buns "Buns") and soft white bread pastries are often sprinkled on top with black and white poppy seeds (for example [cozonac](/wiki/Cozonac "Cozonac"), [kalach](/wiki/Kalach_(food) "Kalach (food)"), [kolache](/wiki/Kolach_(cake) "Kolach (cake)") and [kołacz](/wiki/Ko%C5%82acz "Kołacz")).
The seeds of the Czech blue poppy (food safety cultivars *Papaver somniferum)* are widely consumed in many parts of Central and Eastern [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe"). The sugared, milled mature seeds are eaten with [pasta](/wiki/Pasta "Pasta"), or they are boiled with [milk](/wiki/Milk "Milk") and used as filling or topping on various kinds of sweet [pastry](/wiki/Pastry "Pastry"). Milling of mature seeds is carried out either industrially or at home, where it is generally done with a manual poppy seed mill.

Blue poppy seeds are widely used in [Austrian](/wiki/Austrian_cuisine "Austrian cuisine"), [Croatian](/wiki/Croatian_cuisine "Croatian cuisine"), [Czech](/wiki/Czech_cuisine "Czech cuisine"), [Danish](/wiki/Danish_cuisine "Danish cuisine"), [German](/wiki/German_cuisine "German cuisine"), [Hungarian](/wiki/Hungarian_cuisine "Hungarian cuisine"), [Lithuanian](/wiki/Lithuanian_cuisine "Lithuanian cuisine"), [Polish](/wiki/Polish_cuisine "Polish cuisine"), [Romanian](/wiki/Romanian_cuisine "Romanian cuisine"), [Russian](/wiki/Russian_cuisine "Russian cuisine"), [Serbian](/wiki/Serbian_cuisine "Serbian cuisine"), [Slovak](/wiki/Slovak_cuisine "Slovak cuisine"), [Slovenian](/wiki/Slovenian_cuisine "Slovenian cuisine"), [Turkish](/wiki/Turkish_cuisine "Turkish cuisine") and [Ukrainian](/wiki/Ukrainian_cuisine "Ukrainian cuisine") cuisines.

The states of former Yugoslavia (notably North Macedonia and Serbia, but also Croatia and Bosnia) have a long tradition of preparing poppy seed pastry (štrudla, baklava, pajgle) and dishes (pasta with poppy seeds). In Slovenia blue poppy seeds are used in traditional dishes such as prekmurska gibanica and makova potica.

In [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland"), [Hungary](/wiki/Hungary "Hungary"), [Lithuania](/wiki/Lithuania "Lithuania") and Eastern [Slovakia](/wiki/Slovakia "Slovakia"), a traditional dessert is prepared for the [Christmas Eve](/wiki/Christmas_Eve "Christmas Eve") dinner from poppy seeds. They are ground and mixed with water or milk; round yeast biscuits (*[kūčiukai](/wiki/K%C5%AB%C4%8Diukai "Kūčiukai")* in [Lithuanian](/wiki/Lithuanian_language "Lithuanian language"); *opekance* or *bobalky* in [Slovak](/wiki/Slovak_language "Slovak language")) are soaked in the resulting poppy seed 'milk' ([poppy milk](/wiki/Poppy_milk "Poppy milk")) and served cold.

In [Central Europe](/wiki/Central_Europe "Central Europe"), poppy [strudel](/wiki/Strudel "Strudel") is very popular, especially during [Christmas](/wiki/Christmas "Christmas"). In Germany, Poland and countries belonging to the former Austro-Hungarian Empire, poppy seed pastries called *Mohnkuchen* are often eaten around Christmas time.[[14]](#cite_note-14) Recipes for Mohnstriezel use poppy seed soaked in water for two hours[[15]](#cite_note-15) or boiled in milk. A recipe for [Ukrainian](/wiki/Ukrainian_cuisine "Ukrainian cuisine") poppyseed cake recommends preparing the seeds by immersing in boiling water, straining and soaking in milk overnight.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

#### Jewish cuisine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Poppy_seed&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Jewish cuisine")]

In Eastern European [Jewish cuisine](/wiki/Jewish_cuisine "Jewish cuisine"), pastries filled with black poppy seeds in a sugary paste are traditional during [Purim](/wiki/Purim "Purim"), which occurs exactly one month before [Passover](/wiki/Passover "Passover") and approximately a month before [Easter](/wiki/Easter "Easter"). Traditional pastries include poppy seed [kalács](/wiki/Kal%C3%A1cs "Kalács") and [hamantashen](/wiki/Hamantashen "Hamantashen"), both sometimes known as *[beigli](/wiki/Beigli "Beigli")* (also spelled *bejgli*). Poppy seed hamantashen were the main traditional food eaten by [Ashkenazi Jews](/wiki/Ashkenazi_Jews "Ashkenazi Jews") at Purim until the filling was replaced by other fruit and nut fillings. Poppy seed pastries are common in Jewish bakeries and delicatessens throughout the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States").

### Indian cuisine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Poppy_seed&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Indian cuisine")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/Food-Bati-Posto.jpg/250px-Food-Bati-Posto.jpg)](/wiki/File:Food-Bati-Posto.jpg)

*Bati posto* from West Bengal

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/21/Aloo_Posto.jpg/250px-Aloo_Posto.jpg)](/wiki/File:Aloo_Posto.jpg)

Aloo posto

In [Indian cuisine](/wiki/Indian_cuisine "Indian cuisine"), particularly [Bengali cuisine](/wiki/Bengali_cuisine "Bengali cuisine"), white poppy seeds are added for thickness, texture and flavor to recipes. Commonly used in the preparation of [korma](/wiki/Korma "Korma"), ground poppy seeds, along with coconut and other spices, are combined into a paste added during cooking. Poppy seeds are widely used in many regional Indian cuisines. One dish, originating from [Khulna](/wiki/Khulna "Khulna"), is aloo posto ([potato](/wiki/Potato "Potato") and poppy seeds) which consists of ground poppy seeds cooked together with potatoes and made into a smooth, rich product, which is mainly eaten with [rice](/wiki/Rice "Rice").[[17]](#cite_note-:0-17) There are many variants to this basic dish, replacing or complementing the potatoes with other ingredients, such as [onions](/wiki/Onions "Onions") (penyaj posto), [pointed gourd](/wiki/Pointed_gourd "Pointed gourd") (potol posto), [Ridged Luffa](/wiki/Ridged_Luffa "Ridged Luffa") (jhinge posto), [chicken](/wiki/Chicken "Chicken") (murgi posto), and [prawns](/wiki/Prawns "Prawns") (chingri posto). [Chadachadi](/wiki/Chadachadi "Chadachadi") is another dish from Bengali cuisine and includes long strips of vegetables, sometimes with the stalks of leafy greens added, all lightly seasoned with spices like mustard or poppy seeds and flavored with a [phoron](/wiki/Phoron "Phoron"). One dish involves grilling patties made from *posto*, sometimes frying them (*postor bora*). Another dish involves simply mixing uncooked ground poppy seeds (kancha posto) with [mustard oil](/wiki/Mustard_oil "Mustard oil"), chopped green [chili peppers](/wiki/Chili_peppers "Chili peppers"), fresh onions and rice. Kacha posto bata (uncooked poppy seeds paste) with mustard oil is a very popular dish in West Bengal as well as Bangladesh. Poppy seeds are widely used in [Kashmir](/wiki/Kashmir "Kashmir") as a topping on various breads, especially [kulcha](/wiki/Kulcha "Kulcha").

Poppy seeds, along with [tulsi (basil) seeds](/wiki/Basil#Seeds "Basil"), are added to beverages such as [thandai](/wiki/Thandai "Thandai"), [sharbat](/wiki/Sharbat_(beverage) "Sharbat (beverage)"), [milkshakes](/wiki/Milkshake "Milkshake"), [rose milk](/wiki/Bandung_(drink) "Bandung (drink)"), [almond milk](/wiki/Almond_milk "Almond milk") and *khus khus* milk.

### Pakistani cuisine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Poppy_seed&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Pakistani cuisine")]

Poppy seeds locally called "khashkhash" are an essential ingredient in the preparation of a dish that consists of minced meat balls locally called "Kofta".

## Health effects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Poppy_seed&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Health effects")]

[Allergy](/wiki/Allergy "Allergy") (type 1 [hypersensitivity](/wiki/Hypersensitivity "Hypersensitivity")) to poppy seeds is rare, but has been reported[[18]](#cite_note-pmid16948357-18)[[19]](#cite_note-pmid18564637-19) and can cause [anaphylaxis](/wiki/Anaphylaxis "Anaphylaxis").[[19]](#cite_note-pmid18564637-19)

### False positive drug tests

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Poppy_seed&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: False positive drug tests")]

Further information: [Poppy seed defence](/wiki/Poppy_seed_defence "Poppy seed defence")

Although the drug [opium](/wiki/Opium "Opium") is produced by "milking" latex from the unripe fruits ("seed pods") rather than from the poppy seeds, all parts of the plant can contain or carry the opium alkaloids, especially [morphine](/wiki/Morphine "Morphine") and [codeine](/wiki/Codeine "Codeine").[[20]](#cite_note-usada-20) This means that eating foods (e.g., [bagels](/wiki/Bagel "Bagel")) that contain poppy seeds can result in a [false positive](/wiki/False_positive "False positive") for opiates in a drug test,[[20]](#cite_note-usada-20) although more refined tests can distinguish them.[[21]](#cite_note-nytimes.com-21) To minimize the problems caused by false positives, the [USADA](/wiki/USADA "USADA") recommends that athletes refrain from eating foods containing poppy seeds several days prior to a competitive event.[[20]](#cite_note-usada-20)

## Legal status

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Poppy_seed&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Legal status")]

The sale of poppy seeds from *[Papaver somniferum](/wiki/Papaver_somniferum "Papaver somniferum")* is banned in [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore") because of the morphine content. Poppy seeds are also prohibited in [Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan"), primarily because of the risk that viable seeds will be sold and used to grow opium poppies.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

China prohibits spice mixes made from poppy seed and poppy seed pods because of the traces of opiates in them, and has since at least 2005.[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24)

Despite its present use in [Arab cuisine](/wiki/Arab_cuisine "Arab cuisine") as a bread spice, poppy seeds are also banned in [Saudi Arabia](/wiki/Saudi_Arabia "Saudi Arabia") for drug control reasons.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

### International travelers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Poppy_seed&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: International travelers")]

Travelers to the United Arab Emirates are especially prone to difficulties and severe punishments.[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27)

In [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore"), poppy seeds are classified as "prohibited goods" by the [Central Narcotics Bureau](/wiki/Central_Narcotics_Bureau "Central Narcotics Bureau") (CNB).[[28]](#cite_note-28)
